2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-200). This chapter records the dedicated substrate
5	% on which the abelian-group instance of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
6	% (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) rests. The genuine residual
7	% sorry at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} L235 is
8	% the abelian-group law on the relative Picard quotient
9	% \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\); descending tensor product of
10	% line bundles to that quotient requires a tensor-product operation on
11	% the underlying carrier and an inverse for invertibles, neither of
12	% which is presently exposed at the \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level.
13	% Mathlib at the pinned commit ships
14	% \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
15	% the level of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)), but does not lift this
16	% to a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\). The
17	% chapter describes the substrate
18	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\), records the lift
19	% through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) carrier used by
20	% \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, and pins the consumer
21	% instance back into \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The chapter is
22	% project-side and self-contained: dropping a Mathlib upstream PR is
23	% acceptable but downstream work does NOT wait for the PR to land.
24	
25	% NOTE (planner directive iter-200). The chapter has three
26	% responsibilities. First, it records what the Mathlib API surface
27	% currently exposes at the relevant abstraction levels, what is
28	% missing, and at which abstraction the project-side construction must
29	% land. Second, it spells out the construction of
30	% \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) as a binary operation
31	% on the category \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), its functoriality, and
32	% the four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles it has to
33	% satisfy (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse) in order for the
34	% downstream Picard-quotient consumer to work. Third, it spells out
35	% the canonical lift through the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)
36	% carrier and pins the consumer abelian-group instance on the relative
37	% Picard quotient back into chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
38	
39	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
40	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
41	
42	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
43	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
44	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
45	\[
46	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
47	  \qquad
48	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
49	\]
50	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
51	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
52	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
53	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
56	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
57	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
58	% groups)
59	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
60	% SOURCE QUOTE:
61	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
62	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
63	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
64	% abelian groups defined by the formula
65	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
66	% \end{dfn}
67	%  [\dots]
68	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
69	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
70	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
71	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
72	% topologies  by
73	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
74	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
75	%  \end{dfn}"
76	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
77	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
78	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
79	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
80	homomorphisms.}
81	
82	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
83	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
84	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
85	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
86	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
87	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
88	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
89	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
90	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
91	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
92	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
93	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
94	structure. This is the content of
95	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
96	
97	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
98	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
99	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
100	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
101	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
102	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
103	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
104	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
105	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
106	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
107	\begin{enumerate}
108	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
109	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
110	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
111	    isomorphism classes;
112	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
113	    for \(\otimes\);
114	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
115	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
116	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
117	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
118	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
119	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
120	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
121	\end{enumerate}
122	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
123	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
124	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
125	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
126	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
127	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
128	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
129	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
130	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for
131	\emph{line bundles}, which are flat, that compatibility is \emph{elementary
132	flat-exactness}, not internal-hom machinery. This is exactly the carrier on
133	which the relative Picard group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
134	rests, and it is the subject of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,%
135	sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
136	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
137	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
138	
139	\section{Mathlib API survey}
140	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
141	
142	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
143	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
144	
145	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
146	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
147	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
148	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
149	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
150	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
151	sectionwise tensor product
152	\[
153	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
154	\]
155	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
156	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
157	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
158	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
159	
160	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
162	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
163	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
164	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
165	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
166	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
167	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
168	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
169	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
170	tensor-product operation
171	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
172	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
173	
174	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
175	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
176	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
177	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
178	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
179	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
180	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
181	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
182	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
183	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
184	\(f\), the canonical comparison
185	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
186	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
187	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume, and for line bundles it is
188	elementary flat-exactness already available in Mathlib (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
189	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
190	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
191	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
192	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
193	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
194	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
195	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
196	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
197	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
198	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
199	independently of any upstream timing.
200	
201	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
202	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
203	
204	\begin{definition}
205	
206	\leanok
207	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
208	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
210	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
211	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
212	  \[
213	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
214	  \]
215	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
216	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
217	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
218	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
219	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
220	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
221	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
222	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
223	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
224	  canonical compatibility
225	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
226	     \;\cong\;
227	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
228	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
229	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
230	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
231	
232	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
233	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
234	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
235	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
236	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
237	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
238	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
239	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
240	\end{definition}
241	
242	\begin{lemma}
243	
244	\leanok
245	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
246	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
248	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
249	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
250	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
251	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
252	  \[
253	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
254	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
255	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
256	  \]
257	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
258	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
259	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
260	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
261	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
262	  isomorphisms
263	  \[
264	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
265	    \qquad
266	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
267	  \]
268	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
269	  \[
270	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
271	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
272	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
273	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
274	  \]
275	  and a canonical braiding
276	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
277	  inherited from the corresponding data on
278	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
279	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
280	\end{lemma}
281	
282	\begin{proof}
283	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
284	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
285	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
286	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
287	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
288	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
289	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
290	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
291	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
292	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
293	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
294	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
295	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
296	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
297	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
298	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
299	\end{proof}
300	
301	\begin{remark}
302	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
303	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
304	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
305	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
306	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
307	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
308	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
309	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
310	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
311	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
312	  depend on it.
313	
314	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
315	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
316	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
317	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
318	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
319	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
320	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
321	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
322	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
323	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
324	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
325	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
326	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
327	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
328	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
329	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
330	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
331	
332	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
333	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
334	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
335	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
336	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
337	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
338	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
339	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
340	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
341	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
342	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
343	\end{remark}
344	
345	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
346	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
347	
348	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
349	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
350	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
351	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
352	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
353	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
354	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
355	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
356	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
357	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
358	cover, together with their restriction to the
359	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
360	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
361	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
362	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
363	
364	\begin{lemma}
365	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
366	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
367	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
368	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
369	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
370	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
371	  The canonical comparison morphism
372	  \[
373	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
374	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
375	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
376	  \]
377	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
378	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
379	\end{lemma}
380	
381	\begin{proof}
382	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
383	  The statement is local on \(X\), so it suffices to verify it on affine
384	  opens, where both line bundles are presented by \(A\)-modules \(L, M\) over
385	  a commutative ring \(A\) and the comparison is a map of \(A\)-modules whose
386	  formation commutes with \(L \otimes_A (-)\). A line bundle is locally free
387	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
388	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
389	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\)
390	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}, the
391	  \(\mathtt{instProjectiveOfInvertible}\) instance chain). For a flat module
392	  the comparison is then an isomorphism by ordinary exactness, with no
393	  internal-hom machinery: \(\otimes\) is always right-exact, so
394	  \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity, and flatness of \(L\) makes
395	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve injectivity
396	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
397	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
398	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
399	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
400	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
401	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}): for invertible \(L\), the map
402	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) is bijective iff \(g\) is. Applying
403	  this to the (bijective) structural comparison map for the second factor
404	  \(M\) under restriction yields bijectivity of the full comparison, i.e.\
405	  the asserted isomorphism. The affine isomorphisms are compatible along an
406	  affine cover, so they glue to the global isomorphism.
407	
408	  This is the elementary flat-exactness replacement for the
409	  monoidal-closed / \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\,W\) route recorded in
410	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}: that route was forced only
411	  because the analogous \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability fails for a
412	  \emph{non-flat} module \(F\). Restricting the scope to line bundles makes
413	  the flatness hypothesis automatic, so the comparison is an isomorphism by
414	  module-theoretic exactness alone.
415	\end{proof}
416	
417	\begin{lemma}
418	
419	\leanok
420	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
421	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
422	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
423	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
424	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
425	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
426	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
427	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
428	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
429	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
430	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
431	\end{lemma}
432	
433	\begin{proof}
434	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
435	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
436	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
437	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
438	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
439	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
440	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
441	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
442	\end{proof}
443	
444	\begin{lemma}
445	[Associativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
446	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
447	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
448	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
449	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
450	  bundles. Then there exists an isomorphism
451	  \[
452	    (L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N
453	      \;\cong\;
454	    L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)
455	  \]
456	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently the proposition
457	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N \cong
458	  L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)\bigr)\) holds.
459	\end{lemma}
460	
461	\begin{proof}
462	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
463	  Choose a common affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\)
464	  trivialising \(L, M, N\) simultaneously. By
465	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} every tensor product
466	  occurring on either side is again a line bundle, hence flat, so
467	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} applies and both sides restrict to the
468	  iterated tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules on \(U_i\). There the associator
469	  \((L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i) \otimes_{A_i} N_i \cong
470	  L_i \otimes_{A_i} (M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i)\) is the standard associativity
471	  isomorphism of the tensor product of modules over a commutative ring. These
472	  local isomorphisms agree on overlaps --- naturality of the module
473	  associator against the restriction-comparison isomorphisms of
474	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and therefore glue to a global
475	  isomorphism. Only the \emph{existence} of the global isomorphism is
476	  asserted; no compatibility with a pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
477	\end{proof}
478	
479	\begin{lemma}
480	[Unit law for $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
481	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
482	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
483	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) a line
484	  bundle. Then \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\) and
485	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong L\); equivalently
486	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L)\) holds (and
487	  symmetrically on the right).
488	\end{lemma}
489	
490	\begin{proof}
491	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
492	  On a trivialising affine cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(L\), restriction
493	  commutes with \(\otimes_X\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and the
494	  structure sheaf restricts to \(A_i\) as an \(A_i\)-module; the unitor
495	  \(A_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i \cong L_i\) is the standard left-unit isomorphism of
496	  the tensor product. These local isomorphisms glue to a global isomorphism
497	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\); the right-unit case is identical.
498	\end{proof}
499	
500	\begin{lemma}
501	[Commutativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
502	  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
503	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
504	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
505	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
506	  bundles. Then \(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L\); equivalently
507	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L)\) holds.
508	\end{lemma}
509	
510	\begin{proof}
511	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
512	  On a common trivialising affine cover, restriction commutes with
513	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}); locally the braiding
514	  \(L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i \cong M_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i\) is the standard
515	  symmetry isomorphism of the tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules. The local
516	  symmetries glue to a global isomorphism.
517	\end{proof}
518	
519	\begin{lemma}
520	
521	\leanok
522	[Inverse of an invertible module]
523	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
524	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
525	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
526	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
527	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
528	  bundle. The dual
529	  \[
530	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
531	  \]
532	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
533	  isomorphism
534	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
535	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
536	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
537	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
538	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
539	\end{lemma}
540	
541	\begin{proof}
542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
543	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
544	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
545	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
546	  The contraction map
547	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
548	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
549	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
550	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
551	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
552	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
553	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}
557	
558	\leanok
559	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
560	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
562	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
563	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
564	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
565	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
566	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
567	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
568	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
569	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
570	  \[
571	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
572	    \;\;=\;\;
573	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
574	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
575	  \]
576	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
577	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
578	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
579	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
580	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
581	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
582	\end{lemma}
583	
584	\begin{proof}
585	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
586	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
587	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
588	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
589	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
590	  Direct from
591	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
592	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
593	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
594	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
595	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
596	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
597	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
598	  associativity, unit and commutativity isomorphisms of
599	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
600	  are isomorphisms of line bundles, hence restrict to the subtype;
601	  together with the inverse of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
602	  they equip the isomorphism classes of
603	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with the full
604	  group-law data (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse).
605	\end{proof}

607	\begin{lemma}
608	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
609	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
610	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
611	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
612	  functor
613	  \[
614	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
615	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
616	  \]
617	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
618	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
619	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
620	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
621	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
622	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
623	  canonical isomorphism
624	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
625	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
626	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
627	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
628	\end{lemma}
629	
630	\begin{proof}
631	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
632	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
633	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
634	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
635	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
636	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
637	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
638	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the same
639	  flat-exactness / restriction-compatibility argument as in
640	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (extension of scalars along \(A \to B\)
641	  is itself flat, so the comparison is an isomorphism). The compatibility
642	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
643	  same cover.
644	\end{proof}

646	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
647	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
648	
649	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
650	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
651	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
652	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
653	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
654	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
655	
656	\begin{theorem}
657	
658	\leanok
659	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
660	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
661	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
662	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
663	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
664	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
665	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
666	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
667	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
668	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
669	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
670	
671	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
672	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
673	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
674	  % groups)
675	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
676	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
677	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
678	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
679	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
680	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
681	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
682	  % \end{dfn}
683	  %  [\dots]
684	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
685	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
686	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
687	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
688	  % topologies  by
689	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
690	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
691	  %  \end{dfn}"
692	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
693	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
694	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
695	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
696	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
697	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
698	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
699	  \[
700	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
701	  \]
702	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
703	  abelian-group structure with addition
704	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
705	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
706	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
707	  inverse
708	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
709	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
710	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
711	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
712	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
713	  map
714	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
715	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
716	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
717	\end{theorem}
718	
719	\begin{proof}
720	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
721	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
722	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
723	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
724	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
725	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
726	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
727	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
728	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
729	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
730	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
731	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
732	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
733	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
734	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
735	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
736	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
737	  read on isomorphism classes:
738	  \begin{itemize}
739	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
740	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
741	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
742	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
743	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
744	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
745	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
746	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
747	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
748	  \end{itemize}
749	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
750	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
751	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
752	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
753	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
754	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
755	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
756	  transported from
757	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
758	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
759	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
760	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
761	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
762	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
763	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
764	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
765	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
766	  verbatim.
767	
768	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
769	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
770	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
771	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
772	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
773	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
774	
775	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
776	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
777	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
778	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
779	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
780	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
781	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
782	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
783	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
784	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
785	\end{proof}
